WOMAN'S EXPONENT.

Give her of the fruit of her hands; and let her own works praise her in the gates.—Proverbs, Chapter xxxi, Verse 31.

VOL. 8.		SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, OCTOBER 1, 1879.		No. 9.


For the EXPONENT.
AT LAST.

There must be something we desire to gain,
	A recompense for pacing to and fro;
Some sweet fruition which we would obtain,
	Or greater wisdom, that we fain would knew;
And this is why we toil on, and endure—
	Do battle 'mid life's trials, storms and strife;
Because we feel that, there is something sure
	That we shall find, within a higher life.

But heavy cares and burdens on our way
	Confuse, and weary us, and we forget
Ofttimes, the blessings given day by day,
	The goal, towards which our faces we have set,
Else we should onward press, stemming the stream,
	Facing the current with an effort grand;
Nor sleep upon our oars, or careless dream,
	Lest we lost sight of Eden's promised land.

But in life's summer time so passing sweet,
	When all around seems blooming gay with flow'rs,
We're apt to shrink from duties we should meet,
	And linger—dallying in wayside bowers,
Tlil we're reminded, by the setting sun,
	Th[-]t time, for idlers, will not, cannot wait,
And we resume the task, erstwhile begun,
	Perchance to murmur at our adverse fate.

Lucky if only sweets we gathered, while
	We sought for pleasures on the broad highway;
For sometimes serpents charm us to beguile,
	And lead our footsteps very far astray.
And we must wander in a wilderness.
	In doubt and fear, not knowing what to do,
The sweets we tasted turn to bitterness,
	And thorns and briars, pierce us through and throngh.

Till some good angel takes us by the hand,
	Leading us out of sadness, darkness, night,
And we begin to see and understand,
	That we had wandered far, from truth and light.
'Twere better if we never turned aside,
	But labored diligent with constant care,
Stern duty for our motto, faith our guide,
	Patience our helpmeet—and our watchword, prayer.

Ye who are toiling in youthful prime,
	See that you sow some good, some precious seeds,
Or make some record that will be sublime,
	Engraven in the archives of geod deeds.
For these alone true rest and peace can bring,
	A bounteous yielding from the harrowed past,
That will like glory, round you ever cling,
	Diffusing happiness unto the last.

But if we would life's lessons wisely learn,
	And find that peace for which our spirits long,
The immortality for which we yearn,
	Then we must out of suffering, grow more strong.
There is a something pure for which to live,
	There are bright sunbeams that will ever gleam,
And joys supreme, that heaven alone can give,
	That will exceed our fairest, brightest dream.

September 20, 1879. [handwritten] E. B. Wells	EMILE.


[Column 1]
HOME AND HAPPINESS.

	How to make home happy is a question
worthy of deep thought, and should be in
the minds of all young ladies. There are
so many little things that add to the happi-
ness of a home and fill it with light and
beauty.
	A lady's nature may readily be judged
from the arrangement of her home. If
upon entering a home we find flowers, books,
pretty, not necessarily expensive; pictures,
beautiful and useful little articles of fancy
work, we can easily tell that there is within
this home an industrious, refined, gentle
creature, and we instinctively turn towards
such a one with feelings of affection, and
perhaps love, without ever seeing her. Her
very surroundings tell us where her
thoughts are most, and we know her mind
by studying her handiwork. "A true art-

[Column 2]
ist," one might say as she looks around and
sees the beautiful little mats, cushions, ti-
dies, wall ornaments of autumn leaves,
ferns, grasses, shells, &c.
	It is so easy, so enjoyable, to decorate
one's home in a real cheerful manner, and
it lies within the reach of every human
soul to do this. The best, the brightest or-
nament is a person possessing a peaceful,
contented mind. Where the mind is con-
tent the face is always beautiful, for an ex-
pression of happiness lights up the coun-
tenance and renders the most plain really
pretty. Who with any love for nature, or
art, could rest content with four bare white
walls always staring them in the face? It
relieves the mind, it rests the eyes to have
something pretty to look at. Nothing could
be more delightful to the young and active
than to take rambles through the canons
and meadows during these long summer
days, and gather bunches of the golden
brown and red autumnal leaves, then of an
evening, when the hard day's work is done,
we can sit and weave these carefully
pressed leaves into crosses, wreaths, clus-
ters, and many strange devices, to orna-
ment the walls. Autumn leaves are a
pleasure in every respect, and in the long
winter evenings, as we look upon the pret-
tily arranged leaves that make our sur-
roundings look so warm and cheerful, smil-
ing down upon us, the pleasure of picking
them is recalled—how with dear friends
we roamed over hill and dale and sought
these charming treasures; how we laughed
and chatted as we walked along, stopping
now and then to reach in some dangerous
place to gather a few leaves, which from
their very position seemed more beautiful
than all the rest. Oh, how often do we
value hard sought treasures, though not
nearly as beautiful, more than those found
with little seeking! Yet so it is; we value
the moonlight more than the starlight be-
cause the moon only comes at certain inter-
vals, and the stars are always there. Yet
if, like Jupiter, we had four moons shining
down upon us, we would value the stars
more, because they are farther away. Oh,
ever-changing soul, why not be content with
the near and constant, instead of seeking
that which, seemingly so beautiful and true,
may prove so very false.
	What queer looking individuals we would
be if we had plain, white faces, void of ex-
pression. As the smile and animation light
up our faces, so do the pretty ornaments
light up our homes.
	How nice it is to be able to make little
presents to our friends and companions, and
surely nothing could be more acceptable
than a piece of one's own handiwork. We
love the gift simply for the sake of the
giver, and therefore a little piece of work
is often valued more than the costliest
present, because the donor "did it herself."
When you make a gift, have it as much
like yourself as possible, and it is sure to
be loved. If you are a poet, give me a
poem; if a painter, give me a picture; if a
florist, send me flowers. Then the gift con-
tains the soul of the giver, and you love
and cherish it almost as you would love and
cherish the being it represents,
	It is very nice to have a home filled with
the costliest ornaments of art, such as,

[Column 3]
statues, pictures, vases, &c., but how few
there are who can afford these things, yet
there are none who cannot afford to deco-
rate their home, with the work of their
own hands. Too much time is spent now
on one's personal appearance. To be sure,
we cannot be too careful about making our-
selves neat and pretty, but for a young lady
to devote all her time and intellect on ar-
ranging her costume is simply absurd; yet
there are many who do this and whose
only thought is "How can I make myself
fashionable and attractive?" Alas! what a
mistake, that a pretty face and dress will
do this. If you really wish to be attractive,
decorate your face with smiles, your mind
with knowledge and learning, and your
person with good manners; then every one
will love and admire you. A well-stored
mind is sought after for the ideas it can
impart, a cheerful disposition for the hap-
piness it spreads over all who come in con-
tact with it, for it gives life to those who
are near, just as the sun gives life to the
things it shines upon. Such a mind and
nature is far more attractive than the pret-
tiest face, and the consciousness of making
others happy is sure to bring happiness to
one's self.
	[unreadable handwritting] CAMELIA.


TRUTH.

	Truth, then, is not always agreeable?
Indeed it is not. Truth, taking it as a
whole, is not  agreeable. Every  man,
woman and child dislikes it. There are
agreeable truths and disagreeable truths,
and it is the province of discretion or
sound judgment to make a selection from
these, and not to employ them indiscrim-
inately. Speaking the truth is not always
a virtue. Concealing it is very often jud-
cious. It is only when duty calls upon
you to reveal the truth that it is commend-
able. A tale-teller may be a truth teller;
but every one dislikes the character of a
person who goes frm one house to another,
and inter-communicates all he sees or hears;
we never stop to inquire whether he speaks
the truth or not. He is perhaps all the
worse for speaking the truth, for truth is
particularly offensive in such cases, and
never fails to set families at variance. Si-
lence is discretion, and concealment of facts
is judicious.


ANTIDOTE TO POISON.

	If a person swallows any poison whatever,
or has fallen into convulsions from having
overloaded the stomach, an instantaneous
remedy, most efficient and applicable in a
large number of cases, is a heaping tea-
spoonful of common salt, and as much
ground mustard, stirred rapidly in a tea-
cupful of water, warm or cold, and swal-
lowed instantly. It is scarcely down before
it begins to come up, bringing with it the
remaining contents of the stomach; and lest
there be any remnant of the poison, how-
ever small, let the white of an egg or a tea-
spoonful of strong coffee be swallowed as
soon as the stomach is quiet because these
very common articles nullify a large num-
ber of virulent poisons. - Medical Brief.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT. ﻿


R. S. REPORTS.

	Synopsis of the General Retrenchment
Meeting held in the Fourteenth Ward As-
sembly Rooms Sept. 13, 1879, Coun. E. R.
Snow presiding. After the usual exercises
of singing, prayer and reading of minutes,
Miss E. R. Snow addressed the audience
on the importance of a spiritual and intel-
lectual education, the necessity of mothers
having great wisdom, because they give
character to the child, and that woman's
work was of as much importance as man's.
Our young people marry and go out to form
new settlements - how much they stand in
need of intelligence and the spiritual as
well as mental training they can obtain
through these associations if they are dili-
gent. She made some excellent remarks
upon faith and upon other subjects. In
concluding she expressed a wish that the
sisters who were not in the habit of ad-
dressing that meeting would rise and speak.
Acting upon her suggestions, Sisters Tuft,
Luce, Wheeler, Hudson, Louie Felt,
Griggs, Due and several others spoke
upon the principles of the Gospel, and re-
lated incidents and experiences calculated
to strengthen each other in faith and good
works.
	At the close of the meeting Miss E .R.
Snow said, " I feel this has been a sweet
feast to me this afternoon."

EDITOR EXPONENT:
	A report of the first Conference of the
Relief Societies in  Sanpete Stake of Zion,
held at Manti City on Monday, May 18.
We were blessed with the presence of Sis-
ters Eliza R. Snow and M. I. Horne, who
were making a visit through the county,
holding meetings and organizing Primary
Associations.
	The Conference was called to order by
Sister M. A. P. Hyde, President of Stake
R. S. There were present on the stand,
Sister Eliza R. Snow, M. I. Horne, M. A.
Hyde and her counselors, Elizabeth Allred
and Anna Larsen, also the Presidents of
the several Societies of the Stake Presi-
dent Petersen, his Counselor, Brother Mai-
ben, and many others.
	The reports of the several Societies were
read, which showed that the sisters had
been alive to their duties and energetic in
assisting in the work of God.
	Sister Hyde expressed her thankfulness
for having the presence of the sisters from
Salt Lake City on such an important occa-
sion, and made many excellent remarks on
plural marriage, bearing testimony; also
exhorted us to be careful that our works
might be accepted of the Lord, &c., &c.
	Sister Snow then addressed the congre-
gation on home manufacture and on becom-
ing self-sustaining. Asked the sisters if
they were preparing for Babylon to fall.
Told them to take hold of silk manufactur-
ing, and plant out mulberry trees, and pre-
pare to make their own apparel. Spoke of
having a Hospital that could be controlled
by those of our own faith, that there could
be a place where the young girls could be
taught to administer herbs in faith, and
become good, efficient nurses and under-
stand the human system.
	She then organized a Silk Association,
with Rebecca Wareham, President, Mary
Lowry and Maria Weibe, Counselors, Alvira
C. Cox, Secretary, Edith Billings, Treasurer;
and all the members of the Relief Societies
and Y. L. M. I. Associations as members of
the Silk Association; also appointed an
acting agent in each settlement.
	President Petersen then expressed his
gratification with the proceedings of the
Conference and the remarks that had been
made. Spoke to some extent on home
manufacture. Asked God to bless Sister
Snow and all the sisters in their labors to
build up the kingdom of God, and all the
Saints with love and union.
	Afternoon meeting. President M. A.
Hyde organized the Y. L. M. I. Associa-
tions in a Stake capacity: Helen Armstrong
was chosen President, Christina Willertsen
and Matilda Delain, Counselors, Viola Van-
Cott, Secretary, Sophia Andersen, Treasurer.
	Sister Horne then addressed the congre-
gation upon the training of children. Said
it was very necessary for mothers to have
the Spirit of God to direct them, that they
might be enabled to teach their children in
the spirit of the Gospel, and fit them to be-
come celestial beings. Told the mothers to
teach them the principles of the Gospel be-
fore they arrived to  years of accountability.
	A number of other sisters spoke; all felt
highly edified with the instructions given
by our worthy Sisters Snow and Horne.
They were sufficient, if observed and put
into practice by the people, to qualify them
for an exaltation in the celestial kingdom
of our God, which is hoped will be our
happy lot.
	The Second Quarterly Conference of the
Relief Societies of Sanpete Stake was held
at Ephraim City, Monday, Aug. 18.
	The congregation was called to order by
the President, Mary A. P. Hyde. The
usual opening exercises of singing and
prayer were offered. The reports of the
several Societies, from their first organiza-
tion were read, also the Stake report, and
accepted. 
	It was surprising to all present to hear
the amount that had been donated for dif-
ferent purposes since the organizing of the
Societies, most of which were organized in
the year 1868. They have donated to the
local poor, $57,698 38; to the P. E. Fund,
$4000 43; to the Temple, $2,126 69; for
sundries and stationery, $695 30; Mission-
aries, $281 00; have stored 3,022 bushels of
wheat.
	After the reading of the reports, the
President of each Society, represented her
respective Society as being in a good con-
dition, and all the sisters being united in
the interest of the Society. A number of
other sisters spoke upon different subjects,
but principally upon union and home manu-
factures.
	Pres. E. R. Snow was presented to the
congregation, also the officers of the Relief
Society of this Stake, and unanimously
sustained. Elder Henry Beal, Counselor
to Pres. Petersen, was present, and urged
the sisters to go on as they have been do-
ing, and stated that they would outstrip
the brethren in union and in carrying out
the United Order.
	The choir sang. Benediction. Ad-
journed until Nov. 18.
	Previous to our Conference the people of
the whole Stake were blessed with a visit
from Sisters E. B. Wells and Elizabeth
Howard, who kindly made a tour through
the county, holding meetings and speaking
most appropriately to both old and young.
	Their remarks were spirited, full of truth
and intelligence, and plainly showed that
their whole hearts were for the welfare of
the human family, and for the accomplish-
ment of God's purposes upon the earth.
	Their remarks to the children were so
kind, plain, and so fitted to their tender
minds, that they will leave an impression
for good that will remain to the end of the
lives of many; and will be like seed sown
upon good ground, or bread cast upon the
waters that will be seen after many days in
the character of noble men and women.
	The Primary Association of this place is
in a promising condition, under the guid-
ance of their amiable and kind President,
Sister Sarah Ellis, and Counselors Sisters
Patrea Hitchcock and Miss Christena Lar-
sen.
	They held their First Conference the 31st
of Aug. They have gathered $2.40 for the
temple, and are now improving the oppor-
tunity of gleaning wheat to reserve for
famine. They have also remembered Sis-
ter E. R. Snow's instructions to plant white
beans, and have acted upon it, and each
member is to give a quart this fall to add
to their store of grain.
	They are preparing for famine with all
the ambition and energy that the humming-
bee, or ant, does for winter; and may God
bless them with plenty in the time of need
is the prayer of
	Your sister in the Covenant,
		FANNY KOFFORD, Cor. Sec'y.

		MILLARD STAKE REPORT.

	At a mass meeting of the ladies of Mil-
lard Co. held Jan. 28, 1879, the Relief So-
ciety of the Stake was organized, the fol-
lowing sisters being unanimously sustained
as officers of the Board, viz.: Belinda M.
Pratt, President, Delilah K. Olsen and Sarah
L. Partridge, Counselors, D. K. Olsen, Sec-
retary.
	Our first Quarterly Conference convened
in the 1st Ward Meeting House, Fillmore,
Feb. 23, Pres. B. M. Pratt presiding. Pres-
ent of the priesthood, Pres. I. N. Hinckley,
Edward Partridge, and J. V. Robison; the
Bishops and a number of other brethren
attended by invitation; also a large attend-
ance of the members. After the usual
opening exercises Pres. Pratt stated the
object of the meeting, and desired the re-
ports of the different wards be presented,
which were read by the Secretary.
	Pres. Hinckley and others addressed the
sisters, referring to various principles of
the Gospel connected with the Relief Socie-
ties. 
	Sister B. M. Pratt then made a few clos-
ing remarks, expressing a desire to assist
both in temporal and spiritual works, also
her entire satisfaction with the efforts of
the sisters. Conference adjourned for three
months.
	Statistical and Financial Report of the
Relief Society of Millard Co. for the year
ending Feb. 23, 1879. Statistical: Mem-
bers enrolled, 488; Average attendance,
162. Financial: Property on hand 1877,
41,754 49; Receipts to this date, $1,568 39.
Disbursements, for charitable purposes,
$187 96; Temple, $31 67; Expenditures,
$69 63; Property on hand to this date,
$2,745 63; Wheat, cash price, $290 99;
Total, $3,325 88.


LITERARY WORK FOR YOUNG
LADIES.

	NEPHI, Sept, 19 1878.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	The Young Ladies Mutual Improvement
Association meets every Friday. Our
exercises consist in lectures from the Bible
and Book of Mormon; also songs, select
reading and recitations. We have edited
a paper in our Association, three years, the
name of the paper is "The Young Ladies
Journal." It is issued weekly; contains
original articles, and selections upon relig-
ious subjects.	Respectfully
					SADA HOYT, Secy.,

[For the EXPONENT.
A LITTLE SONG.

There is a song, a tender song,
	Its words are veiled in foreign tongue
But on its cadence floats along
	The sweetest burden ever sung.

I know not what its words may be,
	But oh! I know the music's power;
How sweet! how deep! Its charm for me
	Will haunt my heart forevermore.

This little song of a far clime,
	One voice unto my ear hath taught;
My heart-strings thrill beneath its chime;
	That voice with sweetest magic fraught.

No other song, no other voice,
	Hath e'er such charming in its tone;
It bids my spirit faint, rejoice,
	Yet chimeth with life's saddest moan.

O tender heart! that holds the song,
	O tender voice! that makes the spell;
I know the many thoughts that throng,
	The words that lips will never tell.

And when hereafter you will sing,
	Through twilight, wafts of song and hymn,
This one will come, and with it bring
	A memory, though faint and dim.

Of far-gone, pleasant summer days;
	You'll think of one who once was here;
Wandering content 'mid rural ways,
	To whom this little song was dear.

				AUGUSTA JOYCE CROCHERON.


[For the EXPONENT.
A FEW HINTS ABOUT FLOWERS.

	Repot your Calla's now and give them
plenty of sunshine and water if you wish
early winter blossoms. In my modest
collection of house-plants my Calla's hold
supreme sway as my favorites; though I
am quite partial to my bright colored
geraniums and heliotrope for they (the
geraniums) do brighten up one's rooms so
on a dull winter's day when all without is
cold and white and cheerless. I rather
think every body has a hobby of some sort
and I freely conf[-]ss house-plants is mine;
and a real nice hobby it is too. I don't
think there is any work (if I may call it
work) that I enjoy, about the house so much
as trimming, watering and attending to my
house-plants. I give my lilies rich soil
with plenty of sand to make good drainage
and hot water in the saucer every morning;
and O, how they repay one for a little care
with great white waxy blossoms, graceful
foliage and delicious fragance. I prefer
east windows if possible for my flowers,
though south, or west will do nicely
especially in the winter as then they are not
likely to have more sunshine than they
need. For vines I have English, German
and Kenilworth ivies, or mother of thous-
ands, some call the latter. I must not
forget to mention the Maraunda vine which
is lovely to train about the windows, or
around pictures; it bears a beautiful little
bell-shaped flower in white and purple
which makes it more attractive to me, than
either of the others: though all or any of
them are nice to train about the walls and
windows. They must be kept moist and
given plenty of light and they will do
almost anything you want them to in the
way of twining.
	The Kenilworth ivy looks very pretty in
the pot with your lilies or other tall plants,
and grows so quick it soon covers the earth
and pot. And I am quite sure if any of the
readers of this article will try the experi-
ment they will be satisfied with the result.
The leaves of the English ivy need to be
washed with soapsuds about once a month,
if you wish it to thrive in the house: its
dark green leaves are a pretty contrast to


the German ivy, or Maraunda vine which
ismuch more delicate in shade and tendril
A very pretty idea is to make yourself or
get some handy young man to make for
you, a wooden cross ten or twelve inches
high, and stick it in the pot with some one
of your vines, and they will twine about it
so thickly you will hardly be able to see
the wood at all, and will be tempted to
think you have a cross of living green.
The Maraunda is especially pretty for
this purpose.			ROSABEL.


[Column 1]
ADUMBRATED PORTRAIT.

BY HANNAH T. KING.

	Sketches of character, traced from the
"mind's eye" of one who believes with
the eminent poet, Pope, that "the noblest
study of mankind is man."

STARS

That have passed across my orbit, shedding
in their transit a stray ray or two on my
path of life—spirits, that have passed be-
fore me and are enshrined within the tem-
ple of my heart forever!

CHARACTER.—1ST.

	Presence, carriage and bearing majestic,
and Siddons-like manners, gracious, cour-
teous and conciliating, yet humble and un-
assuming; features of a high and lofty
character, forehead capacious, eyes, those
"windows of the soul," grey and lustrous,
large and pupilling, capable of that com-
plex expression which a fine grey eye alone
exhibits, denoting a cultivated, intellectual,
penetrating, observant soul! shadowing
forth by their brilliancy the jewels of the
casket, yet so passionless, so searching, that
if one had anything to conceal they would
recoil from their quiet scrutiny; a mouth
displaying the firmness and purity of the
character, by the compression and delicacy
of the chiseled lips.
	Yet withal, this being was so mild, seem-
ingly so raised above human weaknesses,
soaring ever above the waves of life's tem-
pestuous ocean, that "'twas an 'ark," where
many a weary "dove" sought and found
repose, found rest, found happiness and
peace; and the loftiness, which almost re-
pelled by its grandeur, melted when we
saw that eye look liquid, that mouth en-
compassed by the workings of emotion at
the sorrows of another, and we loved this 
being the more when we discovered, amid
all its superiorities, it was still "one of us!"
A manly mind, but, dearer still, a truly
feminine heart, was the summary of this
rarely found character!
	Religious in the best sense of the word,
without cant, without Pharisaical assump-
tion, without display, nothing gloomy,
nothing ascetic, nothing exclusive—the
loving, compassionate, charitable Spirit of
the Divine Founder of Christianity, was the
model after which it was cast.
	Conversation condensed and appropriate,
every word telling, with a cheerfulness, and
playfulness, that won all with the sweetness
of their healthy tones. In filial duty, and
in every relative affection, exemplary! In
friendship warm, loving and sincere, ready
at all times to stretch forth a helping hand,
whether to nurse, to comfort, to cheer, to
smoothe the dying pillow, to sustain the
drooping soul, by holding forth the light of
revelation with the simplicity, humility
and love of a disciple of the Savior; a faith
nothing could shake, firm, relying and
child-like.

[Column 2]
	Were there no shades in this bright char-
acter, do you ask? 'Twas human, and that
is ever imperfect; but let not the eye of de-
voted friendship search out, or the hand
trace the shadow upon such a dial; for none
were more alive to the humbling traits of
humanity, none more truly felt that the
gold must have its alloy, none in greater
humility would exclaim: "By the grace of
God I am what I am!"
	Ever possessing a noble, independent
mind, doing good to all with the means
possessed, in prosperity grateful, in trial
submissive and resigned, kissing with
meekness the rod of the Great Father of
the universe, knowing there is a "silver
lining to every cloud." In sickness still
maintaining the same cheerfulness, the
same playfulness, the same resignation, and
in death—yes, alas! alas! in death, that
hour of trial and of truth, joyfully trium-
phant, dying as the erring should die, with-
out parade, without display, alone resting
on the "Rock of Ages." Why fall my
tears? They are purely selfish; no, not all
selfish, they fall over a bright page of mem-
ory, which friendship, reverence and love has
forever turned down on the tablet of my
heart.

(To be continued.)


ALFRED TENNYSON.

	Alfred Tennyson, D. C., is a popular
English poet, the third son of George Clay-
ton Tennyson, rector of Clayton Parish.
He was born at Somerby, in Lincolnshire,
in 1809. He was educated at Trinity Col-
lege, Cambridge, where he obtained a prize
for an English poem in blank verse on
"Timbuctoo," in 1820. He was still an
undergraduate of Trinity Collage when he
published a volume entitled, "Poems,"
chiefly lyrical, in 1830. In 1833 he
published another volume of poems, includ-
ing among others, that exquisite thing,
"The May Queen." (As I write this, the
dear old song and music comes back to me,
when so free and careless I sang it so often
in days of Lang Syne). He also wrote
about this time "The Palace of Art," "A
Dream of Fair Women," and others, which
compared with his earlier efforts, show
great improvement in richness of thought 
and beauty of imagery. His "Princess: A
Medley," is an admirable poem. "In
Memoriam,, is a beautiful tribute to the 
memory of his friend, Arthur H. Hallam.
"Maud" and, "Enoch Arden" are especially
liked by all poetry lovers, also "Idyls of the 
King." Those were published, respective-
ly, in 1859, and 1864.
	Tennyson succeeded Wordsworth as poet
laureate, in 1851.
	The Quareterly Review says of Mr. Ten-
nyson's poetry:—"After the descriptive
beauty of the laureate's verse, perhaps no
quality of it is more highly praised than its
measure," and truly the purity and delicacy
of its movements is almost beyond praise.
Though certain common excellences may
make a plausible resemblance, yet in truth,
no contrast is greater than that between the
jeweled beauty of Mr. Tennyson's style and
the crystalline effulgence—the "non imit-
able [-]ulgur"—of the great ancients. Ten-
nyson has resided on the Isle of Wight for
some years.
	In 1870 he published the "Holy Grail."
The readers of the HOME are doubtless
familiar with his later poems, so I will not
take the space to enumerate them.
					OLIVE HARRINGTON.
	AMERICAN HOME.

SALT LAKE CITY, OCT. 1, 1879.


DO WOMEN EARN MONEY?

	From the way women are referred to, gener-
ally speaking, one would think they were not
entitled to anything, except what they ob-
tained by favor.  Recently a gentleman ob-
served, "Oh, yes, I know that women are giv-
ing a great deal of means for this and that in
their organizations, but where does it all come
from?  Out of the husband's pocket."  Just as
much as to say woman, the wife, the mother,
has no right to anything.  It all belongs to
him, her lord, her master.  It is time the ta-
bles were turned and the other side of the pic-
ture shown.  "One story is good until another
is told" is a homely proverb, but nevertheless
it is a true one.  It is time that women them-
selves estimate the value of their own work,
at any rate appreciate the worth of their own
lives.  How many wives toil from early morn
until late at night, and even into the "wee
sma' hours," and it is scarce recognized?  Per-
haps, were the husband interrogated, he might
say, "Oh, she is a prudent, good woman, she
obeys me, she keeps her place," and so on, but
how few comparatively will say, "Yes, she has
worked as faithfully as I have to accumulate
this property, to make the home, to educate
the children.  And yet, in how many instan-
ces this is really the case.  We remember dis-
tinctly when quite a little girl, of hearing a
clergyman say," My wife never earned a cent
in all her life," and we know, too, that this
good, faithful wife was a complete slave; that
she did more actual work than half a dozen
college-bred gentlemen like himself, and had
more brains.  Do any of you readers know
what is expected of the wife of a clergyman in
a country village or small town?  Do you
know what it is to be the wife of a Bishop?
There is a strong similarity.  This excellent
woman did her own household work, kept his
clerical broadcloth in repair, waited upon him
as if she was his servant instead of his wife,
and was complained of when she was not
dressed to receive company, because, forsooth,
he had his sermons to write, and it took him
four days in a week; and when he was in his
study and his wife had her housework, mend-
ing, making, knitting, baby-tending and all to
do he would call to her every few minutes to
hear this, or that he had written, or suggest
ideas, or look up Latin and Greek he had for-
gotten.  But upon no account could he be dis-
turbed when he was in his study, for their
bread and butter depended upon it.  And so
he preached the very sermons her fertile brain
helped him to write, and dressed with scrupu-
lous nicety, and walked in his garden when
his head ached: but she bent over the baby's
crib, or the hot cooking stove, tired, weary,
and often sick at heart, while the Reverend
arried off the honors, and the young ladies
and the old maids admired his genius and won-
dered at his plain, domestic wife, and thought
she did not half appreciate him.
	And so it is--this man whose wife was a treas-
ure  in every way, finely educated, the daugh-
ter of a clergyman herself, was shut up in the
house from year to year, planning, scheming,
toiling, heart and mind in a constant strain,
to know how to economize, and live genteelly,
and feed, clothe and educate the children, on
the very small salary her husband had the
honor of earning; but "she," poor thing,
"never earned a cent." 
	There are many such records as this all
around; there is no need to go back for them.
We do not allude to the class of women who
are a dead weight in the community, who live
only for pleasure, and are always complaining
of "ennui," but to wives and mothers who
toil from morn till eve, rearing families, and
are so much occupied with work that they
have scarcely time to call upon a friend, or
answer a letter.  Do they earn any money?
Has their time and labor any real money value?
We insist that it has, moreover, that the hus-
band who says whatever she uses is his, does
her an injustice, to say the least.  Does he ask
his wife when he wants to make a trade, or a
purchase, or treat a friend, or bestow charity,
or give his note, or put his name to a note for
a friend; and yet the last two concern the wel-
fare of his family, it may be very materially.
No, of course he don't tell his wife; it is not
her business, she wouldn't understand it if he
did, and it would be a source of annoyance.
The money is his, the property he has earned,
so he thinks and sometimes says, and he gives
her what she needs, so far as he can afford.
	Out of these very simple things great evils
have grown, women who had not the moral
courage to ask for what they wanted, and we
must confess it takes a great deal, but who
have sufficient sense to realize they have some
rights, that they cannot and dare not contend
for, have often been led to deceive, to do things
"on the sly," as it is termed, and justify them-
selves in doing so, feeling that they have a
perfect right to do it; but this is not all - what
effect does a course of this kind, a lifetime of
deceit, have upon the children?  We speak
advisedly when we say there have been in-
stances of this sort, and they are not rare.
Every person who knows anything of the laws
of human nature, must be aware that such a
course would be disastrous to the best interests
of a family, and that there could be no real,
true harmony in the domestic circle where
there was deception, fear and want of confi-
dence.  Where does the blame fall?  Probably
in nine cases out of ten it would be attributed
to the wife.  This  is  a subject  that  much
might be said upon.
	The wife who performs her home-duties
faithfully has no time to earn money outside
her home while she is rearing her children,
but she earns money at home.  Certainly wo-
men earn money.  If a person comes into the
house to do the work, that person must be
paid in money, or its equivalent.  Yet men
will say without a blush,  "My wife never
earned a cent"  And if she happens to be-
stow anything for "charity's sweet sake," it is
not hers, it came out of her husband's pocket.
	We are thankful there are noble men, who
do not entertain these narrow views; and as
women become conversant with business mat-
ters, step out of the kitchen now and then, as-
sociate together in a way calculated to elevate
and benefit each other, study the best methods
of life, health and vigor of body and mind, do
their own thinking, seeking to become good
mothers and rear children who will be an
honor to their parents, having sound bodies
and vigorous intellects, these evils which exist
today may in a great measure be eradicated.
Another and better day will dawn upon wo-
man,  and we believe even in the "magical
matter of money."  The position of women
with relation to money is one which might be
discussed by  intelligent  women advanta-
geously.
	One of the lessons taught in an association
of women should be to use money wisely and
make better investments than the extravagant
adornments of their own persons.  There are
women, who, with the resources they have,
could help much in enterprises that are for the
general good, and thus materially benefit so-
ciety; but when it is expended in tawdry, or
expensive jewelry, elaborate costumes, and
luxuries which only undermine the health, it
is a curse instead of a blessing. The econo-
mizing and saving money from useless ex-
penses and superfluous waste, is one of the
relative duties of woman to the household.
The enormous risks and lavish outlays of busi-
ness men leave this care devolving upon wo-
men, if they have any forethought, or desire
to be a true helpmeet.  One thing we always
notice, that those who are most splendidly
dressed, seldom have anything to give; their
pockets are empty and their hearts are desti-
tute of generosity or benevolence.  Women
who dress simplest and work hardest, as a
rule are the ones who are most useful and most
generous in every public need or enterprise.
	It is necessary, in order that woman may
fulfill the sacred mission given her in this
Church, that she should be highly endowed
with opportunities for culture, and that she
should comprehend her own needs, her true
value; that she make no reckless expenditure
of time, or of bodily or mental power, nerve,
or brain force, in order to ensure an enlightened
motherhood, and that she may walk side by
side with man, her brother, as a co-recipient of
divine blessings and influence, without any
"cruel limit" to her sphere of usefulness, or
education, mental or spiritual, for "the man is
not without the woman nor the woman with-
out the man in the Lord."  But to conclude;
if men will transact the business and fill the
posts of honor and importance, earning the
money, women should be able to judiciously
save, and saving is equivalent to earning.
	How much would a man have to pay out, for
having his socks darned, and his linen kept in
order during his lifetime, leaving out all other
items?					E. B. Wells


HOME AFFAIRS.

	ON SUNDAY Sisters M. I. Horne and E. S.
Taylor visited Sandy Station and organized a
Primary Association, Mrs. Margaret Maginnis
President.
	ON FRIDAY and Saturday, Sept. 19 and 20,
Sisters Eliza R. Snow and Elizabeth Howard
attended the Quarterly Conference of the Re-
lief Societies, Young Ladies' M. I. A. and Pri-
mary Associations of Weber Stake of Zion,
Mrs. Hattie Brown presiding.
	ON THURSDAY, Sept. 18, Miss E. R. Snow
visited Kaysville, held two meetings and or-
ganized the children of each district into a
Primary Association. Mrs. Jane Blood was
appointed President of the first district, and
Mrs. Mary Ann Macpherson of the second dis-
trict.
	WEDNESDAY, SEPT. 24, Sisters M. I Horne,
S. M. Heywood and Phebe Woodruff attended
the Annual meeting of the Relief Society of


[Column 1]
Brinton, Big Cottonwood Ward, Mrs. Eliza-
beth Howard presiding. In the evening the
sisters held a general meeting, which many of
the brethren of the ward attended.

	TO-DAY IS the opening of the Territorial
Fair; great pains have been taken by the sev-
eral committees to make it a success in every
way, and we sincerely hope it will be well pa-
tronized, and help to promote the industrial
pursuits and home manufactures of the Terri-
tory. There is a creditable representation from
the women in their line of work and orna-
mentation.

	MRS. MARTHA B. Young has returned from a
visit to her relatives in the East. She traveled
down in company with Lieut. Willard Young,
and returned alone. Mrs. Young spent most
of the time with her relatives in Mt. Holly
New Jersey, her native place, but she also
visited her brother in Philadelphia. She
enjoyed her visit very much, and her health
improved during her journey, but her friends
did not wish to hear anything about the Gospel.

	THE WOMAN'S Conference of this Stake of
Zion was held in the Fourteenth Ward Assem-
bly Rooms Friday and Saturday last, Mrs.
M. I. Horne presiding. The time was almost
entirely occupied with the verbal and written
reports of Relief Society, Young Ladies' and
Primary Associations. Saturday afternoon
Pres. A. M. Cannon and his Counselor Joseph
E. Taylor was present and set the board of of-
ficers of the Relief Society Stake apart to their
office and calling.

	AN ORDINARY-SIZED weekly newspaper has
been started in Logan, called 'The Logan
Leader." Its editor is Mr. Frank Cannon, son
of Hon. George Q. Cannon. We have received
No. 3 of Vol. I, and think the young editor
deserves great credit for making the paper so
interesting, We are glad Logan has a local
paper, as it is a thriving town and can surely
make it a success financially. Newspapers
help in a great degree towards educating the
masses of the people.

	WE ARE pleased to be able to announce to
our readers and the many warm friends of
Mrs. Zina D. Young, that she arrived in Salt
Lake City on Sunday, Sept. 14, after an ab-
sence of ten weeks, much improved in health.
Miss Susa Young also returned with her. These
two ladies visited San Francisco and the Sand-
wich Islands, remaining on the island of Laie
some little time. We expect to have some in-
teresting things to publish in our next concern-
ing their tour.

	WE WISH the sisters who are storing grain to
report as soon as convenient after they have
made arrangements for the fall donations.
And every Society, or Ward, where the sisters
own a granary, or bin, for storing their own
wheat will please report particulars,—the ma-
terial of which it is built, the size and capacity,
and the cost, and also how the means was ob-
tained, whether by donation, or otherwise.
Report what quantity of the wheat has been
gleaned, and whether the Society own land on
which they raise wheat, or beans. Please give
full particulars in brief.

	THE RELIEF Society of the Fourteenth Ward
of this city have completed a granary for stor-
ing wheat. It was dedicated on Monday, Sept.
21, by Pres. Angus M. Cannon and his Coun-
selor, Joseph E. Taylor. Mrs. M. I. Horne,
her Counselors, Secretary, and members of the
Society were present. After the dedication,
refreshments were served in the building. I[-]
is large enough to hold 2,000 bushels of wheat,
and cost about $350. Who will say women

[Column 2]
have not energy and enterprise, when from
North to South and from East to West these
granaries are being constructed and filled with
grain by Mormon women? "By their works
ye shall know them."

	THE LAND OF SHINEHAH, a poem of eight
pages, written by Elder O. F. Whitney, is be-
fore us. It was composed during his stay in
Kirtland, when on a mission to the Eastern
States, and is dated, Nov., 1877. The sub-
heading says: "The writer of the poem is re-
presented as contemplating the Kirtland of to-
day and contrasting it with its condition forty
years ago." It contains some very tender pas-
sages, embodying truths and events of actual
occurrence. Joseph the Prophet coming to
Kirtland, the people leaving there, its down-
fall, the Temple, the mobs, afterwards the
flight of the prophet, and other things
are poetically woven into this sublime poem.

"Fair Shinehah! thy land so desolate—
A wounded bird deserted by its mate,
The plumage from its bleeding body torn,
A picture is of loneliness forlorn."

	These pathetic lines will speak to the hearts
of those who remember Kirtland in its former
glory and pride.

	OUR SISTER, Mrs. L. M. Hewlings, returned
on Thursday from a visit to California, where
she has been spending the summer, in excel-
lent health and delighted with the climate and 
the eternal verdure of the Southern coast. It
is hard times for the poor, especially in San
Francisco, and suicides are common. Mrs.
Hewlings lectured twice in Santa Barbara on
"Life among the Mormons," and disappointed
her audiences by speaking in their favor. She
gave them a brief outline of the origin and
growth of Mormonism, the small handful from
which had sprung this wonderful people, whose
principles and institutions had become first a
national, and then an international question.
Facts such as she portrayed, coming from one
who was not herself a Latter-day Saint, elicited
much surprise, and many came to converse
with her and make inquiries, which gave her
a good opportunity to speak in behalf of a peo-
ple many of whom she is intimately acquaint-
ed with, and for whom she entertains the high-
est esteem. Mrs. Hewlings is on her way East,
and only called enroute to say good bye to the
dear ones here. She has resided in this city
for seven years.

	REPORT OF the re-organization of the Ladies'
Relief Society of the Twelfth Ward. A meet-
ing was held on the 13, of July 1879. in the
Twelfth Ward School House for the purpose of
organizing the Ladies' Relief Society. Present
Bishop Alexander Pyper, Couns. John
Druce and Leonard G. Hardy; also Pres. D. O.
Calder, of Salt Lake Stake, Bishop L. W. Hardy
and the members of the ward. After a highly
interesting address from Bishop A. C. Pyper
upon the harmony and unity that should
cha[-]acterize the members of the Ward in their
social and organized capacities; he dwelt upon
the interest and care he, his counsel and the
leading men of the Ward had taken in the
appointment of such ladies as would qualify
themselves to proform the responsiblb duties
of their respective callings. He had a personal
interview with these ladies who cheerfully
consented to take upon themselves and ac-
quiesce in the appointment.
	The Bishop and his Counselors, assisted by
Prest. Calder and Bishop L. W. Hardy, set apart
the following named sisters, President; Mrs
Julia Hooper, Cou[-]selors Mrs. Jemima Midg-
ley and Mrs. Eliz[-] Hooper; Secretary, Mrs.
Ellen Winder; Treasurer, Mrs. Martha Wells.
The meeting adjourned to a future day to further

[Column 3]
consult in relation to the appointment of lady
teachers.
			ROBERT CAMPBELL, Secretary.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


IN MEMORIAM.

	Died in the Seventeenth Ward of this
city on the morning of September 18, Elizabeth
Isabel infant daughter of Erastus F. and Ann
Maria Whitney Hall. Deceased was born March
19, 1878. and had been almost a constant
sufferer; She has entered upon a higher and
purer life; and gone to join the innumberable
host of little ones who sing praises to God in
that beautiful land of peace and rest, where
all suffering ceases and there is no more pain
nor sorrow.

	DIED, in the 17, Ward of this city Sept. 25,
1879, Eliza Rose daughter of Richard and
Mary Ann Matthews aged 10 years and three
days. Diseased was a loving and affectionate
child, and had great faith in the efficacy of
prayer, and the administrations of those hold-
ing the priesthood for the healing of the sick.
We sympathize deeply with the bereaved par-
ents, and trust their hearts may be comforted
by the Holy Spirit.


CORRESPONDENCE.

						CLIFTON, IDAHO.

EDITOR EXPONENT:
	On the 7th of August S[-]sters E. R. Snow, M.
I. Horne, S. M. Heywood and S. M. Kimball
visited our settlement and held two meetings,
during which we all enjoyed ourselves exceed-
ingly, this visit being their first.  They gave
good  timely instructions, and the sisters
feel more encouraged to persevere in the cause
they have espoused.  Sister E. R. Snow or-
ganized a Primary Association, and the chil-
dren are very much interested in their meet-
ings, and they will long remember the good
instructions given them by the sisters.  Many
of the brethren attended the meeting, and
were pleased with what they heard.
	We also have a Y. L. M. I. Association, and
the young sisters seem to appreciate their
privileges in meeting together for mutual
benefit.
	Several copies of the WOMAN'S EXPONENT
are taken here and duly appreciated.  We
think it a great blessing and help.  Every
mother in Israel should take it and read it.
	We tender our thanks to the sisters from Salt
Lake City for their respect, confidence and ex-
cellont advice received.
D. E. DUDLEY, [-]or. Sec'y.
WHITLEY, ONTARIO, CANADA,
Aug. 22, 1879.
ESTEEMED SISTER WELLS:
	I feel under obligations to you for your kind
ness in forwarding to me regularly the EXPO-
NENT.  Not only have I been comforted in its
perusal, but it has enabled me to entertain my
lady friends through reading from its co umns.
In conversing with the women of this country,
I find them far behind the times.  Woman's
rights being a question that has not agitated
the masses here, and many view it is a step-
ping up into a plane that they are not designed
to occupy by Deity.  As I am quite a woman's
rights advocate, I have some amusing times,
w[-]en I tell them, that many of the existing
evils of the age if ever remedied must be ac-
complished by them, or their sex, they stare at
me.  I state as my firm belief, that the evils of


intemperance, corruptions in office, and the
eradication of the social evil, can and will
only be accomplished by and through the in-
fluence of woman, and men who view woman
in her true light and the noble sphere God has
designed her to occupy. The minutes of a
mass meeting held by our women last fall, with
the bold and fearless statements there made
and the testimonies given, have aided me very
much in my labors this summer. The idea
that we practice polygamy only for selfish
gratificatiou I am happy to say has forever ex-
ploded in the minds of many with whom I
have conversed.
	I notice wherever I go in this dominion the
young ladies are very numerous, and in watch-
ing the young folks out walking, I am sur-
prised to see the girls generally form compa-
nies of two or three, or groups, to the exclusion
of the young men. Now, having some slight
knowledge of human nature, I was surprised
at witnessing this. On inquiry of one of my
cousin's, residing in Walberton, Bruce Co., I
was informed that most of the young men's
characters are usually so tainted that honora-
ble girls will not be seen in their company.	*
*[-]*	My cousin is raising a family of eight
children, chiefly boys, but neither he nor his
wife can fully sense the danger that exists
around them. To-day darkness covers the
minds of the people.
*		*		*		*		*		*
	With a hope that success may crown your
every [-]ffor[-] to emancipate woman and assist
in bringing her up to her proper standard and
sphere, is the desire of your brother in the
cause of regeneration.
							W. H. S.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
GOOD MANNERS.

BY BLANCHE BEECHWOOD.

	In this chapter I purpose entering more
fully into details, and give some of the es-
tablished rules and regulations in society at
the present day. All of us who have kind
hearts and generous impulses desire to be
sufficiently acquainted with the forms of
social etiquette to ensure the respect of
those with whom we associate by main-
taining our own.
	I know that it is customary among ladies
to like a little gossip about other people's
affairs, and from this very source much
evil springs, and we all profess to despise
it, yet it really seems to render a person
for the time attractive, but upon mature
thought, instead of winning friends it
causes people to draw very disadvantageous
conclusions in regard to those who carry
scandal. This is one of the evils which
should be discarded by us as a people, and
discountenanced, that it may not be incor-
porated in the characters of our children.
	Most persons whose opinion is worth re-
garding have sufficient good, sound com-
mon sense and penetration to see through
the flimsy veil of the envious, or the slan-
derer. It is said by those who have the
best opportunities for observing, that this
is the age of scandal; that there never was
a time when newspapers and journalists
pandered to a low and vitiated taste as at
present. It has ceased to be extraordinary
to deliberately defame and recklessly as-
sault the reputation of those who come be-
fore the public. The continual recurrence
of a thing blunts the susceptibilities; and
we become accustomed to look upon it with
less horror, however dreadful it might at
first have seemed. 
	Every writer, journalist, editor, author,
whatever position in a literary way he or

[Column 2]
she may occupy as regards the public press,
should be careful of the privileges extend-
ed to them, and have some high and sacred
notions of honor and esteem for their fel-
low-beings.
	A writer in the Washington "Republi-
can" says of a certain class of writers: "It
is their office to defame virtue and despoil
worth to feed on the failings of the good,
and fatten on the follies of the weak. Vile
themselves, without a sentiment of honor
or decency, they cannot endure to see
others respected for traits they do not pos-
sess, or beloved for conduct of which they
are incapable. Hence they make the es-
tate of purity the prey of their piracies
and the object of their plunder. Nothing
is so sacred as to deter them, and no emi-
nence is beyond their attack. Is there a
man who stands high in the estimation of
the public, by reason of the excellence of
his character and the quality of his endow-
ments, they rest not until they have
smirched the one and disparaged the other
by the fiendish devices of inuendo and in-
sinuation, which constitute the weapons of
the guilty ambush they keep in perpetual
reserve for those they dare not openly as-
sail for fear of popular resentment. Lives
there a woman whose fair fame transcends
the plane of ordinary attainments, because
of special attributes, accomplishments and
graces, all the precedents of successful cal-
umny and falsehood are ransacked for sug-
gestion of means to depose and humiliate
her, without subjecting the authors of the
detraction to the punishment they deserve."
	It would be well for us all if we could
cultivate that "charity that thinketh no
evil." Perhaps if we understood each other
other better we should be more careful in
speaking of another's weaknesses, or even
faults, and ready to make excuses for those
whom we now unknowingly condemn.
	Society can never be in its relations and
bearing upon our lives what it ought to be,
until the love of gossip is dispelled; and
those who wish to elevate its tone and
maintain a high standard of excellence
should frown it down and refuse to listen
to it. Dr. Holland tells us that "the cure
for gossip is culture."
	We shall now review the rules for visits,
or ceremonious calls, which Mrs. Ward
gives, and trust our readers have sufficient
intuition to know what is best suited to
them. "American men are excused from
morning calls, because their days are occu-
pied with business as a general rule; but
in order that they may be remembered by
those who entertain, their cards represent
them and are left by some member of their
respective families, or by some acquaintance
calling.	*	*	When a gentleman is not
admitted the first time he calls, he leaves
his card for the married lady of the house,
one for her husband, both turned down,
and one folded acress the middle for the
remaining members of the family—daugh-
ters and sons." Upon subsequent occasions
he leaves one card so folded as to imply it
is left for the family. "After any invita-
tion he calls, or sends a card; or, if a mar-
ried man, his wife calls and leaves his card
with her own. If one of the cards bears
their names together, as, 'Mr. and Mrs.
——,' this card turned down is left for the
lady, if she is not receiving; and one with
the husband's name alone is left for the
host, not turned unless he has called in
person.	*	*	*	No lady leaves her own
card upon a gentleman, nor a card bearing
her name with that of her husband. If
guests are stopping in the house, cards must
also be left upon them; or, if calling upon
guests, where you do not know the host

[Column 2]
and hostess, you must inquire if the ladies
are at home, and, not being admitted, leave
cards for the host and hostess, as well as
for the guests. *	*	* There are many
people who give evidence of lack of proper
instruction in their youth, by making use
of the house of those who are strangers to
them, with as much freedom and as little
courtesy towards its occupants as if it were 
a hotel. *	*	* After an interchange of
cards the acquaintance drops, unless fol-
lowed by an invitation upon one side or the
other. When a first invitation is not ac-
cepted, and no reason is given for it other
than that expressed in the usual form of
regret, the invitation ought not to be re-
peated. Among the people of the highest
cultivation it is binding to show ones' ap, 
preciation of a first invitation by a cordial
acceptance, and by allowing nothing that
can be controlled to prevent one from go-
ing. Still circumstances may be such as to
make it impossible, and then an informal
note of explanation is courteous." These
are what are termed general calls, as also
are calls made upon persons on one's visit-
ing list who have been absent from their
homes, either for a long time, or for a lim-
ited time, as for the summer. In these
cases the younger calls first upon the elder,
or where the ages are about the same, those
who return first in the autumn call first up-
on those who arrive later, unless there has
been some remissness during the previous
year, when the one who owes the custom-
ary visit after an invitation calls first, with-
out reference to age, or time of return.

(To be continued.)


ESSAY ON FAITH.

		READ AT A MEETING! OF THE Y. L. A.

	As faith is the first principle of the Gos-
pel, it is necessary to inquire what is faith?
the Scriptures tell us that "faith is the as-
surance of things hoped for, the evidence
of things not seen;" for example if I should
tell you that by going to a certain place
you could obtain a piece of gold or a pearl
of great beauty, and consequently you go
and find it, you manifest your faith by your
works. Thus it is when we obey the
Gospel, certain blessings follow; but if we
say we believe and do not obey then is our
faith vain. It is like the body without the
spirit, dead. It was this living faith, my
young sisters, which caused your fathers
and mothers to obey the Gospel, in their
native lands, and homes; in the midst of
scorn and persecution through faith they
left homes and kindred and all they held
dear and embarked on the mighty ocean
tossed upon the waves and billows for weeks
and months, far from the sight of land sea-
sick and weary, but still trusting in the
arm of Jehovah to bring them safely to a
haven of rest; and many of them had never
known the trials and hardships of life, leav-
ing weeping friends distracted with grief,
who had not faith, and did not understand
the purposes of God. Through faith they
traveled across the plains with the slow
wearisome travel of oxen—but still more
wearisome traveled with hand-carts;
think of it my young sisters: your fathers
and mothers traveling over a thousand miles
drawing a hand-cart, with their rations and
bedding, cooking utensils, clothing and etc,
many of them  with little children; fording
rivers up to their waists and toiling
through miles of heavy sand, and yet by
the camp-fire at night their songs of praise
resounded to God, for the principles of faith
were planted in their bosoms; they had an


assurance of things not seen; by faith they
arrived in these valleys; they were not then
the rich fertile valleys you now see. By
faith they subdued the sterile, barren soil
and through the blessings of God succeeded
in making the wilderness blossom as the
rose, it may yet be said of us in succeeding,
generations as it has been said of Moses;
they struck the soil, and it brought forth
abundantly, but it has taken years of ardu-
ous toil—toil that now tells on the con-
stitution of those who have passed through
it.
	Time would fail me to tell you of the
trials of our faith caused by crickets, grass-
hoppers, droughts, floods, and also by the
persecution of our enemies; but in all we
are an exceedingly blest and happy people;
and through faith we intend to grow and
increased and spread abroad till like Abra-
ham of old, of our increase there shall be no
end. 
	Now my dear young friends you need
not think that because we have done so
much there is nothing for you to do, you
have got to spread abroad; you need
not think that the Territory of Utah is
going to hold the children of Zion you
will have to build up new Colonies.
	It is for our young people as they are
called upon, to leave fathers and mothers
for Zion's stakes must be strengthened
and her "cords lengthened." "Give us
room that we may dwell, Zion's children
cry aloud." The Scriptures are fulfilling
right before our eyes; the wicked are
afraid that we will take away their name
and nation, but the prophecies must be
fulfilled through earth and hell should dare
oppose.
	Therefore contend earnestly for the faith
delivered in these last days to your fathers
that you may be enabled through faith to
help bring to pass the purposes of Jehovah;
and may the quickening influence of the
Spirit of God rest upon the children of
Zion, that it may be as a living fire within
them bringing forth much fruit unto right-
eousness, then can you say with the Poet.
		"In danger in trials and afflictions
		We have shared with the worthies of yore,
		That we at truths banquet may join them,
		When errors great conflict is o'er."
							MARY FERGUSON.
	Spanish Fork, Sept. 20, 79,


WOMEN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
		LETTER FROM LULA.

	GRANTSVILLE, TOOELE CO., U. T.,
						Aug, 12. 1879.
DEAR AUNT EM:
	What a world of good is often the result
of some simple change in the life of a weak,
suffering mortal! change of air, change of
diet, change of scenery and of associations;
these little things often work wonderful
cures. My year-old boy and myself have
realized the blessings and benefits of those
changes, to a marvelous extent, during the
past week.
	For more than a month our visit to the
country had been talked of. But being un-
decided as to what length of time the visit
might extend, (as I still am,) I could not
leave home until after the August election.
	No, although longing, almost starving
for a breath of fresh country air, I prize my
right of franchise too highly, to throw away
lightly chances of voting for good men as
officers, and run the risk of bad ones being
elected without having made use of the lit-
tle power I hold in the matter. But the
next morning after election day, "baby"
and I, under the tender guardianship of
kind, careful "papa," set out on a forty-five

[Column 2]
mile journey and an extended visit to our
friend "Mary," of Grantsville.
	Providence seemed to take special charge
of us during the day. Every thing that
transpired appeared to be in our favor.
And although our poor baby, was quite sick
and weak, the journey though somewhat
fatiguing to him, proved beneficial rather
than otherwise.
	"Papa" only remained with us three
days, just to become convinced that "baby"
was really improving, and with careful nurs-
ing would, through the mercies of God, be
all right. Then he returned to the busy,
dusty city, leaving "baby" and I to recruit
at leisure, in care of trusty and most hos-
pitable friends.
	Now, as I sit in the cool, green shade,
writing a promised letter to our dear EXPO-
NENT, listening to the dashing and splash-
ing of a beautiful mountain stream and the
song of birds among the branches over head,
I feel a profound sympathy for ever so many of
my friends and acquaintances, who need
just such changes as have come to me in
the last week, but are not privileged as
I am.
	I think we should have one more set of
philanthropists in Salt Lake City. We
have the Sunday School advocates, who see
to it that the children and young folk's
have their pleasant "outs" and jolly times
at least once a year; and we have the "Com-
mittee for the Old Folks Excursion," who,
for some years past, have each summer,
given our aged friends and the infirm and
the very poor among us, a first class ex-
cursion. But there is an intermediate
class, belonging neither to the young nor
the old, who live in a sort of social starva-
tion, who mingle with no crowds of pleasure
seeking and find no time nor opportunities
for recreation, no seasons for refreshment
and rest. I refer to that class of over anx-
ious over tasked wives and mothers who
"stay at home forever." I will not here
enter into details, or attempt to explain the
'whys and wherefores" in the case. We
all know that the class of wives and mothers
I have named is too long. And we also
know that if any living creature needs
changes, such as I mentioned at the begin-
ing of my letter, it is the child-bearing
mother. If they are beneficial to others,
they are of twofold benefit to her. I hope
we may some day have a committee or com-
mittes, either self commissioned or au-
thoritatively appointed, to work up this
subject and make it one of general, in-
terest, as others are, and must be if success-
fully handled to promote the general good.
	Here let me say, should the ideas ad-
vanced in the last paragraph chance to reach
the ears of the Anti Polygamy workers of
Utah, they may gather from them the be-
lief that I have been making a complaint
in behalf of neglected polygamous wives;
which would be in keeping with the other
beliefs expressed by them in relation to
our people and their systems—an entire
mistake. As far as my acquaintance
extends among both polygamous and mon-
ogamous Mormon families, the wives of
the former enjoy far more freedom, and
many of them are less tied at home by
family cares than the latter. *	*	*
	Polygamy, when properly understood
and lived in, certainly lightens the bur-
dens of home work and home cares more
than any other domestic arrangements can.
	This is but one of the least of its advan-
tages; but I should think that almost the
dullest intellect might comprehend this
simple fact. While the higher and holier
blessings pertaining to the order of plurality
of wives, we cannot expect those to under-

[Column 3]
stand who have not received the light of
the Gospel. Nor can we, who have been
favored with a testimony of its truth,
estimate or appreciate its full value.
	For myself, and for my sisters in the
Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints,
my fervent prayer is that we may ever
have abiding with us, sufficient of the
Holy Ghost to enable us to honor the princi-
ple and practice of plurality of wives,
under all circumstances. Then I believe,
we shall be able to prove ourselves faithful
in all things.
	The people of Grantsville are lively, good-
natured, and sociable so far as I can judge
by the small opportunities I have had of
mingling with them. This branch of the
Relief Society is doing well, both officers
and members being ambitious to understand
and perform all their duties. An excellent
spirit is manifested at their meetings.
The hot, dry season has had its deleterious
effects here as in other localities, grain
and fruit crops being scarcely half as good
or abundant as in former years.
	There are but small chances for gleaning
or saving up grain in any way here this
season.
	Oh! may the Saints be more united and
more diligent in serving God than they
have hitherto been, that His blessings may
not be withheld from them.


[For the EXPONENT.
AMAZONIAN LILY.

	Every body is interested in something
wonderful, and as I have seen something
truly wonderful, I will endeavor to present
it to you through my eyes.
	The Victoria Regia, the Amazonian lily,
has caused a great deal of excitement and
pleasure, and thousands of people have
been to view this gigantic flower, which
has been in bloom in the Golden Gate con-
servatory for three nights. The queen lily
is the only one in the Golden Gate, and is
considered to be the queen, and is the largest
flower in the floral kingdom. It occupies
apartments by itself. The leaves are 6 x 8
ft. in diameter, and are a dark rich green
with many veins. They have raised rims
and one would suppose the leaf would be-
come filled with water, but nature has pro-
vided for all emergencies. There is a
groove in the centre of the leaf which
causes the water to run off. There is a
tube that furnishes air to nourish this plant.
It is of the Nymthaera class, having no
bulb. This wonderful flower, when in
bloom, is a marvel of loveliness, and is an
interesting study to those fond of botany.
	When the bud appears upon the surface of
the water it resembles a wild rose in shape,
but very much larger. It requires three
days for the bud to mature after it appears
on the water, and in the evening at 9 p.m.,
it bursts forth with a loud report, and an
odor resembling the pineapple pervades the
room. In its first stage it is a cream white
and its petal of a thick waxy nature. In
its second stage it is blush pink, and in its
third, rose red; it blooms but three times
and always at night. It is eight times as
large as a large rose and is very double. In
its third stage it does not remain open so
long, but withers and dies, and the seed
begins to grow and is like a pea, and inside
the pea is a dry powder.
	A raised platform has been constructed
to afford a better view of the pond, on the 
surface of which repose these gigantic cir-
cular leaves with their curious raised rims.
The appearance of the leaves on the water
is far more striking when viewed by an


artificial light than when seen in the day
time. The leaves are capable of sustaining
a weight of eighty pounds. There are two
leaves on exhibition in the fair, and every
day a young miss of this weight is placed
upon the leaf. During the blooming of this 
queen lily we were blessed with the finest
moonlight nights I ever saw here, and it
was warm and delightful. The conserva-
tory and the path leading to it were gaily
lighted with Chinese lanterns, and just in
front of the Lily was an electric light
	Thousands of people walked out, a dis-
tance of six miles and the street cars were
more than three times full, but always
room for one more. We had to make
changes as we would if going to Europe,
and rush more to get a seat; I made eight
changes, but I saw the wonder if I did get
jammed nearly to death. There doesn't
seem any place for little folks in a crowd.
I'm very anxious to know who is here writ-
ing for the EXPONENT, and allow me to 
compliment her for her well written article.
	The park in which is the conservatory
ends at the Cliff and will no doubt be fully
described by your correspondent, and she
may have anticipated me in my descrip-
tion, and have given you a more accurate
delineation of this wonderful Amazonian
lily.
	I think the last number of the EXPONENT
super excellent.
								NELLIE.
	San Francisco, Sept. 5, 1879.


72		WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


SIR ROWLAND HILL.

	Sir Rowland Hill, who recently died in
England was the man who introduced what
is considered the Post-Office Reform. To
inaugurate this movement he suffered sor-
rows, anxieties, pangs of hope deferred, and
a thousand and one ills such as all, great
reformers in any department of business
have to struggle against and overcome, if 
they ever attain their object.
	Mr. Hill was an almost obscure school-
master, (we are told) when he commenced
his attack upon the Post-Office. He made
himself acquainted with the subject in all its
bearings, and decided in his own mind on
a uniform penny-rate: "but he preferred to
let his idea of uniformity have time to take
hold of the reader's mind before starting
his revolutionary views in their startling
distinctness. This wise caution was amply
justified by the result. As Mr. Hill was
not employed in the Post Office, that depart-
ment rose against him in a body. He was
pooh-poohed by practical men, who knew
their work, as an interloper, an outsider,
an amateur meddler," "a fellow from Bir-
mingham coming to teach people their busi-
ness." His success was great although it
took a great deal of time.
	Sir Rowland Hill was born in 1795, he
wrote his pamphlet when he was fourty-one
years old. It was entitled "Post Office Re-
form." His services to his country and to
mankind can scarcely be overrated; especi-
ally was his work a blessing to the poor,
who under the old system had not the ad-
vantages of postal communication. This 
man like many others, served as a living
example of ingratitude. In 1854, he was
made Chief Secretary of the department.
	"In many respects Sir Rowland Hill may
be cited as an almost typical Englishman,
and especially with regard to the mature
age at which he advanced into public life.
The device of the mulberry tree tardy to
put forth its leaves and at last displaying
both leaves and fruit together, has been
taken by an Italian, but it would indicate


the lives of the vast majority of success-
ful Englishmen at least as accurately as it
did that of Lodovico Sporza.	*	*	"At the
age of sixty-five he was made a kinght
Commander, of the Bath, a distinction hon-
estly earned twenty years eariler." The
life of Sir Rowland Hill was full of honor
and dignity.


Why Cucumber are bitter.—One of the causes
gardeners give for cucumbers being bitter is that
such are grown too slow; that in order to secure
sweetness and crispness, they should be forced to
grow as fast a posible.

	Caution.—Never use for culinary purposes an
earthen dish after the glazing is worn from the
inside. The next coating is litharge of lead. I
can point to one instance of lead-poisoning for life
resulting from ignorance of this danger.—J. D. M.

Canning Fruit.—) have canned fruit for years by
pouring the liquid hot from the stove into the
glass can, first taking the precaution to put in a
spoon. Thus the much dreaded fruit canning is
made easier. Try it, but never forget the spoon.
J. D. M, San Jose.
